claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 16 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 16 and not in approved education or extension period
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Child is 16 and not in approved education or extension period
circumstances: Child is 16 and not in approved education or extension period
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Hello! I'd be happy to help you determine whether you're eligible for Child Benefit. To assess this properly, I'll need to gather some information about your circumstances and your children.

Let me start with the first question:

**How many children are you inquiring about, and what are their names?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hello! I'm interested in finding out if I can claim Child Benefit. I have one child - my son Alex.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information! So you have one child, Alex. 

To help me assess your eligibility for Child Benefit for Alex, I need to ask you some questions about your circumstances.

**First, how old is Alex?**

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you. Alex is 16 years old.

**Next, is Alex still in full-time education or training?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, he's not in education or training at the moment.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information.

**Does Alex live with you?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, he lives at home with me.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you. Before I make my final assessment, let me check the eligibility rules to ensure I have the correct information about age limits and other requirements.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Based on the eligibility rules I've reviewed, I need to clarify one important point:

**You mentioned Alex is 16 years old and not in education or training. According to the rules, Child Benefit is normally only payable for children under 16, OR for 16-19 year olds if they're in approved education or training.**

Since Alex is 16 and not currently in approved education or training, he would not qualify for Child Benefit under the standard rules. However, there is one exception:

**Can you confirm whether Alex has registered with either a government-sponsored careers service or the armed services?** This would allow Child Benefit to continue for up to 20 weeks after leaving education or training.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, he hasn't registered with the careers service or anything like that. He just left school and isn't doing anything at the moment.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you for clarifying that. I now have all the information I need to assess your eligibility.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Summary:**

Unfortunately, you are **not eligible** to claim Child Benefit for Alex at the moment.

This is because:
- Alex is 16 years old
- He is not in approved education or training
- He has not registered with a careers service or the armed services

Child Benefit is typically available for children under 16, or for 16-19 year olds who are in approved education or training. There is a limited exception for 20 weeks if a young person registers with a careers service or the armed services after leaving education, but this doesn't apply to Alex.

If Alex does register with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed services in the future, or if he enters approved education or training, you may then become eligible to claim Child Benefit for him. I'd recommend contacting the Child Benefit Office if circumstances change or if you'd like further advice about your specific situation.

reasonings: Alex is 16 years old and not in approved education or training. According to Child Benefit eligibility rules, you normally qualify for Child Benefit if your child is under 16, or if they are 16-19 and staying in approved education or training. There is an exception allowing 20 weeks of Child Benefit if a 16 or 17 year old leaves education or training and registers with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed services. However, Alex has not registered with either of these services and is not currently doing anything. Therefore, Alex does not meet the eligibility criteria for Child Benefit.
arguments.overall_reasoning: You are not eligible to claim Child Benefit for Alex. While Alex lives with you, he is 16 years old and not in approved education or training, nor has he registered with a careers service or the armed services. This means he falls outside the eligibility criteria for Child Benefit, as the benefit is only payable for children under 16 (or 16-19 year olds in approved education/training, or those in the 20-week grace period after leaving education if registered with a careers service or armed services)."

There is an exception allowing 20 weeks of Child Benefit if a 16 or 17 year old leaves education or training and registers with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed services. However, Alex has not registered with either of these services and is not currently doing anything. Therefore, Alex does not meet the eligibility criteria for Child Benefit.

While Alex lives with you, he is 16 years old and not in approved education or training, nor has he registered with a careers service or the armed services. This means he falls outside the eligibility criteria for Child Benefit, as the benefit is only payable for children under 16 (or 16-19 year olds in approved education/training, or those in the 20-week grace period after leaving education if registered with a careers service or armed services)."
